A secular Jew and a Holocaust survivor, Tom is a cool customer: a louche, assimilated Brit whose astringent view of his ethnicity, and irritation at being forced to opine on Israel at all, ultimately leaves him more vulnerable, not less—hamstrung by his intimacy with a brilliant bigot. The contradictions of their closeness aren’t papered over; the unease is allowed to linger, inflamed and ineradicable.
“Birthright” works under different rules: it can’t subtract Israel, like “Long Story Short,” or take refuge in the past, like “Giant.” But, as heartfelt and skillfully performed as “Birthright” is, in its eagerness to become a healing ritual for the audience it dodges what Dahl, in an acid exchange in “Giant,” deems “the vexing bit.” In Act I, Lev is thrilled by a Talmudic concept: that letting contradictory interpretations coexist is a distinctly Jewish way of encountering Hashem, or God. That’s a beautiful idea, but in drama it can feel like throwing up one’s hands. And for some people, as a Yiddish milkman once put it, there is no other hand. ♦

epub_34.title: Elegy for the Penny
epub_34.text:
Elegy for the Penny
Poems
Elegy for the Penny
By Catherine Bowman
July 13, 2026
What will I give you now
for your thoughts. Without those
two pairs of copper eyes
in my pair of copper loafers,
who will guide the child
feet in me across Heaven’s and Hell’s
streets. How will I learn to look
up instead of to the earth as I
kneel down hoping no one sees,
to scoop the lost and forgotten
from a puddle of mud
in a church parking lot,
knowing that’s what brings luck.
And where will be the mother
digging down in her purse
among the tobacco dregs, coral lipstick,
and half-sticks of Wrigley Spearmint
chewing gum to pull out a gift
for fountains and sacred wells.
To play poker and smell my pennied
fingers. And all that candy.
What could be prettier!
And up in Heaven
in vault after vault,
laid out side by side,
wrapped tight in neat rows,
roll after roll of pinched
bundled blessings,
Lincolns in sleeves
just waiting
to be reawakened.

epub_35.title: Review to Remember
epub_35.text:
Review to Remember
Poems
Review to Remember
By Terrance Hayes
July 13, 2026
Should you need it here is your review to remember
that just above & below the moments of helplessness
lingered good moments with no objective measures.
Take a moment to recall the moments you got most excited:
those little cinematographies of summer, the harvest of park
or parking choreographies, those occasional ethereal
slow-motion evenings. Many moments were dark
comedies about the capitalist dehumanization of security.
The bombs were ubiquitous. You had to keep your limbs
loose until the sunrise became a warm velvet.
At the start & end of the year your Mondays featured
students writing about their bodies,
the only resource anyone can claim for most of life.
Several of the year’s best moments came at the birthday
parties of your cousin at fifty-four & your grandmother at ninety,
though there were moments of pot-boiling melodrama
because your mother chose to miss both parties.
The year in review celebrates several days in the company
of your son, daughter & niece: the twentysomethings:
in April in D.C. & over dim sum in Chinatown at Thanksgiving.
High on the moments in review are the extraordinary hands
of your daughter directing her brother & cousin
as she stages an art exhibit selling her handmade lamps
against the unravelling darkness of the government,
her pale soon-to-be-let-go-by-the-Treasury Department
ex-boyfriend coming & going nervously as handsome brothers
came & went. In March there was this brief, glorious moment
wherein there was not a whiff of Trump-Musk spoiling
the air of your ninth-grade niece’s track meet.
A few memorable moments offered glimpses
of what one can be. At this age the most important thing
your talent & resources can secure is healthy love.
The moments you left the phone plugged
in another room always were more memorable
than when you kept the screen close to your face
making everything feel as nebulous as it does.
Those moments are prized for your attention.
You argued whether one had to lie to dream,
the tensions between charm & narcissism,
& the existence of Time with everyone younger than you.
Your niece, son, & daughter: for Thanksgiving:
remember what it felt like having them in the house
when the bombs are ubiquitous.
Some moments were propelled by fantastic
performances by the offspring of others.
Sometimes a moment suddenly put you
directly inside another moment that cut back & forth
across multiple moments & you got lost
trying to reconnect with twentysomethings
whose childhoods were set around you.
At moments like that, it’s important to know
you’re not alone. Your top ten good moments
of the year include seeing “Sinners” once with your son & once
with your daughter. The central conflict
was between creation & consumption on & off
our screens. Real people were real characters.
This year gave you new perspectives on parenting.
Some moments held the best physical comedy in years.
The less inventive through lines & missteps
had everything to do with gravity. You defined space
in terms of boundaries when space operates outside
boundaries. You defined Time by beginnings & endings
when Time has no beginning & ending.
Remember you only have to be good & clear & kind.

epub_39.title: Letters from Our Readers
epub_39.text:
Letters from Our Readers
The Mail
Letters from Our Readers
Readers respond to Melanie Thernstrom’s piece about a concierge women’s-health doctor, Burkhard Bilger’s piece about the lone-star tick, and Adeline Goss’s piece about the potential connections between frontotemporal dementia and criminality.
July 13, 2026
At a Price
It was hard for me to read Melanie Thernstrom’s piece about fabulously wealthy women spending thousands of dollars on a gynecologist who coaches them on optimizing their orgasms, because it arrived the same week that my town’s small community hospital, in southeastern Vermont, announced that it anticipates having to close its birthing center this year (“
Billionaires’ Vagina Club
,” July 6th & 13th). If that comes to pass, then many women will have to travel out of state, often across long distances, for obstetric care. This week, I also read that the average annual compensation for a C.E.O. of a major national health-insurance company is more than twenty million dollars. I also encountered a poll, no doubt instituted to mark our nation’s two-hundred-and-fiftieth birthday, asking: Are you proud to be an American? I’m thinking about how proud I would be if our country made serious strides to eliminate this level of economic injustice.
Karen Tyler
Brattleboro, Vt.
The Meat of It
Burkhard Bilger’s piece about how lone-star ticks are spreading alpha-gal syndrome, a condition that causes meat allergies, is terrifying and informative (“
The Great Infestation
,” July 6th & 13th). What’s troubling to me, besides the obvious, is the insistence on finding a way to continue to eat animals if your body starts to reject them. “Vegan” may be a curse word to some, but there’s never been a better, and easier, time to be one than now. Brands like Beyond and Impossible make delicious burger patties without meat. Of course, there are critics who say that these products don’t pass their purity tests, but alternative meats have the advantage of not requiring factory farming or breaking you out in a rash. Another plus is that you don’t have to raise cows or pigs only to kill them, just because you prefer the taste of red meat.
Robert Howard
Madisonville, Ky.
Bilger’s article struck a chord with me. Fifty years ago, when I was thirty, I passed out with a swollen face and hives a few hours after eating prime rib at a celebratory dinner. My family doctor, in Tennessee, thought I had an allergic reaction, but he couldn’t tell what had triggered it, and implored me to see a specialist. All the allergy tests doctors later performed came back negative. It was only after I started eliminating foods that I realized my reactions were caused by red meat.
Many years later, I was diagnosed with alpha-gal syndrome. My journey with this condition has entailed visiting the emergency room three times, always carrying an EpiPen, travelling with a card that describes my allergy in several languages, and constantly alerting restaurant chefs and staff that I can have no food that might contain meat by-products or be contaminated by them. Last year, after a lovely dinner in San Francisco that I thought was safe, I was in an emergency room explaining alpha-gal to doctors who had never even heard of it.
I am waiting for the day when there might be a preventive remedy. In the meantime, I appreciate the exposure
The New Yorker
has given to this unusual allergy, which is prevalent in the South and now moving across the country.
Sarah Hunt
Nashville, Tenn.
Perilous Predictions
I was troubled by Adeline Goss’s recent article about Ray Howell, a doctor whose criminal actions may have been caused by frontotemporal dementia (Annals of Science,
newyorker.com/goss-on-ray-howell
, June 11th). Howell’s F.T.D. was linked to a genetic mutation known as a C9orf72 (C9) hexanucleotide repeat expansion. I write from a place of concern for the well-being of those living with this mutation, as I fear that many readers will be left with the impression that there is strong evidence that having it is definitively and consistently associated with profoundly negative characteristics, including criminality. As a statistician who has partnered with A.L.S./F.T.D. advocacy groups and researchers to interrogate the published literature on the genetically at-risk community, I believe that the evidence does not support this impression.
Goss cites a study that found half of F.T.D. patients who were C9-mutation carriers had fixed behavioral patterns, like rigid meal schedules and excessive exercise routines. Research indicates that the majority of mutation carriers will never develop F.T.D., however, so it is unclear how these findings would apply to the broader C9-carrier population. Furthermore, this study should not be used to draw conclusions because its sample size for carriers was very small: only twenty. The authors also describe exercising more than four times a week as excessive. Surely most people would agree that four times a week is not necessarily excessive. What’s more, the authors do not present any formal analysis for the observation, which is based on relatives’ accounts, that the study’s subjects became (as Goss quotes) “bad tempered” if they couldn’t exercise. Finally, the paper acknowledges that the study’s interviewer was not blind to the C9 status of its participants and went in with a specific hypothesis—circumstances that risk bias.
More broadly, it’s also worth remembering how far Dr. Howell’s actions are from the norm: even among C9 carriers who do develop F.T.D., serious crimes of this kind are rare. We should be careful how we communicate the implications of C9 mutations so as not to stigmatize carriers, or discourage them from participating in research or getting clinical care. C9 carriers deserve to be treated with respect—and to know that a sequence in their DNA does not define who they are.
Daniel Brickman
San Francisco, Calif.
•
Letters should be sent with the writer’s name, address, and daytime phone number via e-mail to
themail@newyorker.com
. Letters may be edited for length and clarity, and may be published in any medium. We regret that owing to the volume of correspondence we cannot reply to every letter.
